text.title: Agentic AI Product Principles
text.summary: Core distinctions between traditional AI features and agentic systems, including MVI framing and cost structure shift.

rule.statement: Design agentic products around goal achievement, not task completion.
rule.rationale: Traditional AI is user-triggered and single-model; agentic AI orchestrates multiple models toward an outcome autonomously. Measuring task completion misses whether the agent actually solved the business problem.

list.item: Traditional: single model, user-triggered, task completion, static behavior.
Agentic: orchestrated systems, autonomous action, goal achievement, learns from results.

rule.rationale: Agentic systems need a machine-verifiable definition of success to know when to stop, escalate, or retry. Describing steps instead of outcomes forces the agent into rigid scripting.

rule.statement: Design the human-in-the-loop model before designing the happy path.
rule.rationale: 40% of agentic pilots fail to productionize because they lack a production-grade escalation path. The escalation design prevents production disasters; the happy path is optimistic.

rule.statement: Make agentic behavior explainable: always surface the agent's reasoning to operators.
rule.rationale: Unexplainable autonomous decisions block trust and regulatory approval. Explainability is a product feature, not an engineering afterthought.

section.title: Minimum Viable Intelligence (MVI)

rule.statement: Replace MVP with MVI for agentic products: scope on intelligence level, not feature count.
rule.rationale: Feature count is a proxy for value in traditional products. For agentic products, value comes from the reliability and autonomy of the agent's decisions. An agent that handles 80% of inbound tickets reliably is more valuable than one that handles 100% poorly.

section.p: Cost range: ~$50K (MVI) to $500K+ (enterprise autonomous). Cost structure shifts from development-heavy (70% salaries) to infrastructure-heavy (60% inference + infra).

rule.statement: Match model to task complexity: haiku for pattern application, sonnet for analysis, opus for strategy.
rule.rationale: Model cost is a first-class product metric for agentic systems. Overusing high-capability models on mechanical tasks erodes unit economics.

example.note: Haiku for methodology implementation, Sonnet for code review, Opus for agentic strategy design.
